question_title: How many roll-call votes will the U.S. Senate have held in the 2nd session of the 119th Congress by June 27, 2026?
summary: The Senate had 172 roll-call votes as of June 15, 2026. With 8 session days remaining before the June 26 deadline (June 16-18 and June 22-26, with Juneteenth on June 19 as recess), and a YTD average of ~7-8 votes per week (~1.3 votes/session day), I expect approximately 12 additional votes for a total around 184. The distribution is slightly right-skewed due to a small (~2.5%) probability of a nomination batch or legislative burst adding 15+ extra votes. The post-vote-a-rama slowdown (only 1 vote over June 12-15) pulls slightly toward the lower end, while pre-recess nomination processing could push it higher. Two independent Monte Carlo simulations converge on median 184, P10=178, P90=192.

factors.description: Current count is 172 as of June 15, with 8 session days remaining (June 16-18, 22-26). At YTD average rate, this yields ~12 more votes.
YTD average pace of 7-8 votes/week (1.3/day) is well-established across 23+ weeks of data, providing a strong anchor.
Post-vote-a-rama slowdown: only 1 vote in the 2 most recent session days (June 12-15), suggesting a temporary pace reduction.
Pre-recess nomination processing: Senate often batches judicial nominations before the July recess, which could increase the rate above average.
Confirmed at least 1 vote happening today (June 16) on motion to proceed to S.J. Res. 190, establishing that voting activity continues.
Small probability (~2.5%) of another vote-a-rama or unusual burst (e.g., reconciliation bill returns from House conference), which would add 15-25 votes.

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question:** How many roll-call votes will the U.S. Senate have held in the 2nd session of the 119th Congress by June 27, 2026?

**Current state:** 172 votes as of June 15, 2026 (with at least one more vote in progress today on S.J. Res. 190).

**Key estimates:**
| Percentile | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| P10 | 178 |
| P25 | 180 |
| P50 (median) | **184** |
| P75 | 188 |
| P90 | 192 |

**Reasoning:**
- **8 session days remain** before June 26 (June 16-18 and June 22-26; Juneteenth on June 19 is a recess day)
- **YTD average: ~7-8 votes/week** (~1.3 per session day), well-established across 23+ weeks of data
- **Post-vote-a-rama slowdown** is temporary — the pace dropped to ~0.5/day after the June 3-11 burst but is expected to normalize
- **Pre-recess nomination batches** could push the rate above average
- **Small tail risk (~2.5%)** for another burst event (reconciliation bill return, emergency legislation)

The distribution is slightly right-skewed with most of the probability mass between 178-192.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) How many roll-call votes will the U.S. Senate have held in the 2nd session of the 119th Congress by June 27, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44083)
[List of bills in the 119th United States Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_bills_in_the_119th_United_States_Congress)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the sequential number of the highest-numbered roll-call vote held by the U.S. Senate in the 119th Congress, 2nd session (2026) with a date on or before June 26, 2026. The number is taken from the official Senate roll-call vote list for the 119th Congress, 2nd session: https://www.senate.gov/legislative/LIS/roll_call_lists/vote_menu_119_2.htm . For example, if the highest-numbered vote dated on or before June 26, 2026 is "Vote 00210", the question resolves as 210. If that page is unavailable, the equivalent figure from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reporting the official Senate roll-call vote count will be used.
fine_print: Only roll-call votes (recorded votes) are counted; voice votes and unanimous-consent agreements are excluded, consistent with the numbered list on the resolution page. The relevant value is the maximum vote number with a date on or before June 26, 2026, regardless of when the page is checked. If the Senate holds no further roll-call votes after vote No. 171, the question resolves as 171.

factors.description: Current count is 172 as of June 15, with 8 session days remaining (June 16-18, 22-26)
YTD average pace of 7-8 votes/week suggests ~12 additional votes over 1.6 effective weeks
Post-vote-a-rama slowdown: only 1 vote in last 2 session days (June 12-15), suggesting a pause
Pre-recess nomination processing: Senate often batches nominations before July recess, which could increase rate
At least 1 vote happening today (June 16) on motion to proceed to S.J. Res. 190
Small probability (~2-3%) of another vote-a-rama or unusual burst of votes, which would add 15-25 votes

input.anchor: YTD average of 7-8 votes/week × 1.6 weeks remaining + 172 current = ~184 votes. Monthly rates ranged from 0.85/day (May) to 1.77/day (April), giving 179-186 for 8 session days at those extremes.
input.assessment:
The question asks how many roll-call votes the Senate will have held by June 26, 2026. Current count is 172 as of June 15.

Key analysis:
1. **Session days**: 8 remaining (June 16-18, 22-26). June 19 is Juneteenth/recess. Recess starts June 29.
2. **Pace**: YTD average is ~7-8 votes/week (~1.3-1.6/day). Monthly variation is substantial - May had only 0.85/day while April had 1.77/day.
3. **Recent trend**: After the vote-a-rama burst (36 votes in ~7 days), pace has dropped sharply to ~0.5 votes/day over June 12-15. This is normal post-burst cooldown.
4. **Upcoming business**: Senate is currently processing judicial nominations. A CRA vote (S.J. Res. 190) is in progress today. Pre-recess weeks often see batches of nomination votes.
5. **Monte Carlo**: Two independent simulations converge on median ~184, P10 ~178, P90 ~193.

The distribution is slightly right-skewed because of the small probability of an unusual burst (emergency legislation, large nomination batch). The lower tail is bounded by the current 172 count plus the near-certainty of at least a few votes this week.
input.calibration_notes: My estimate of 184 median aligns well with the simple calculation of 172 + (1.6 weeks × 7.5 votes/week) = 184. The post-vote-a-rama slowdown could pull this slightly lower, but pre-recess nomination processing could pull it higher. These roughly cancel out. The P10-P90 range of 178-193 spans ~15 votes, which reflects the substantial week-to-week variation in Senate activity (May had 4.25/week vs April's 9.75/week).
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether the Senate will process a large batch of nominations before the July recess (could add 5-10 extra votes). 2. Whether any major legislation comes to the floor for amendment votes. 3. Whether the June 22-26 week is actually a full session week or gets shortened.
input.tool_audit: Research sub-agent: Successfully retrieved current vote count (172), monthly breakdown, and vote categories. Senate schedule research failed due to tool unavailability. Direct web fetch of senate.gov returned 403 errors. GovTop provided useful real-time floor information showing today's activity. GovTrack vote 173 page returned 404 (hasn't happened yet). Senate calendar PDF was successfully read.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was very effective at getting the current vote count and historical data. Senate.gov being behind a 403 is a persistent issue - the GovTop aggregator was a useful workaround. The inability to see the actual Senate calendar colors (red vs black dates) in the PDF was handled by cross-referencing with the question text. The Monte Carlo simulation was straightforward and both model variants converged, giving confidence in the result.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Resolution Criteria
The agent fetched the Metaculus question (post_id 44083) which includes full resolution criteria: resolves to the highest-numbered roll-call vote with a date on or before June 26, 2026, from the official Senate roll-call vote list. The factors and assessment correctly engage with this definition. ✅

### Hallucinated Evidence
All six factors' core claims are grounded in the research trace:

- **Factor 1** (172 votes as of June 15, 8 session days remaining): Confirmed by GovTrack API research showing "172 roll call votes" and GovTop votes page confirming Vote 172 on June 15. Session day count correctly derived from calendar (June 16-18, 22-26, excluding Juneteenth). ✅
- **Factor 2** (7-8 votes/week YTD pace): 172 votes / ~23.5 weeks ≈ 7.3/week. Monthly breakdown (Jan: 20, Feb: 23, Mar: 31, Apr: 39, May: 17) confirmed from GovTrack. ✅
- **Factor 3** (Post-vote-a-rama slowdown, 1 vote in last 2 session days): GovTop votes page shows Vote 172 on June 15, Vote 171 on June 11 — only 1 vote across June 12 and 15 (the 2 session days between June 11 and 16). ✅
- **Factor 4** (Pre-recess nomination processing): General behavioral claim, not a specific factual assertion. Reasonable. ✅
- **Factor 5** (Vote happening today on S.J. Res. 190): Confirmed by GovTop real-time data: "at 11:43 a.m., the Senate began a roll call vote on the motion to proceed to S.J. Res. 190." ✅
- **Factor 6** (Small probability of another vote-a-rama): Scenario possibility, appropriately given 0.15 confidence. ✅

### Double-Counting
Factors 1 and 2 draw on overlapping data (both use the vote count history to project forward), but they approach the estimate from different angles — Factor 1 as base count + time remaining, Factor 2 as rate × time. This is mild overlap, not severe double-counting, and the logit magnitudes are reasonable (+1.5 and +1.0).

### Wrong-Direction Factors
All logit signs match their descriptions. Factor 3 (slowdown → negative logit -0.5) and Factor 4 (pre-recess busy → positive logit +0.5) are correctly signed. ✅

### Contradictory Assessment
The narrative assessment centers at 184 with 80% CI [178, 193], which is entirely consistent with the factors and both Monte Carlo simulations (which independently converge on median ~184). ✅

### Tool Audit
Agent's claims match the ground truth metrics. Agent reports senate.gov 403 errors — confirmed (fetch_url 50% error rate, 5/10 errors). Agent reports GovTrack vote 173 returned 404 — confirmed. Agent reports Senate calendar PDF successfully read — confirmed. No meaningful discrepancy.

### Counterargument Quality
Strong. Constructs both a lower case (~175-178 based on May-like lull) and higher case (~195-205 based on nomination batches), with four specific triggers that would change the estimate (UC agreement content, vote-a-rama scheduling, extended recess, actual count by June 18). Not a token gesture. ✅

### Self-Assessment Calibration
Confidence of 0.55 is appropriately modest given: solid data foundation but inability to access actual Senate floor schedule, and genuine uncertainty about the rate regime for remaining days. ✅

### Probability Assessment
Center of 184 with P10=178, P90=193 is well-supported by the evidence. My independent estimate: 172 + ~1.3-1.5/day × 8 days ≈ 182-184 center. The range captures reasonable variation in daily rates (0.85/day in quiet months to 1.77/day in busy months). Two independent Monte Carlo simulations converge on the same distribution. No meaningful divergence from my assessment. ✅

### Anchor Divergence
Anchor is 184 (YTD average × remaining weeks + current count). Final estimate is also 184. No departure to justify. ✅

### Worldview Consistency
No related forecasts or worldview entries found for this question. No contradictions. ✅

No errors found warranting a warn or fail.
